title: Design Elements and Tidying Your Visualizations

yaml.title: Design Elements and Tidying Your Visualizations
yaml.author: Amanda Ng

markdown.markdown:
{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

In this section, we focus on some tips to make your visualizations look polished and purposeful.

```{webr}
#| include: false
pfas_data <- read.csv("data/pfas_data.csv")
birth_data <- read.csv("data/birth_data.csv")
birth_data <- birth_data %>% rename(State = State.Abbreviation)
pfas_data_mean <- pfas_data %>%
  filter(Analyte == "Total PFAS") %>%
  group_by(State) %>% 
  summarise(mean_ppt = mean(Value..PPT.))
pfas_births <- left_join(pfas_data_mean, birth_data, by = "State")
pfas_births <- na.omit(pfas_births) 

total_pfas_data <- pfas_data %>%
  filter(Analyte == "Total PFAS") 

cannabis_data <- read.csv("data/cannabis_data.csv")
cannabis_data <- cannabis_data %>% 
  select(prov, education3, income_recode, community_size, use_freq_rec) %>%
  filter(if_all(everything(), ~ . != -7)) %>%
  filter(if_all(everything(), ~ . != -8))
cannabis_data <- cannabis_data %>%
  # Transform numerical back to categorical representation
  mutate(recreational_use_frequency = factor(use_freq_rec, 
                       levels = 1:7,
                       labels = c("< 1 day per month", "1 day per month", "2-3 days a month", 
                                 "1-2 days a week", "3-4 days a week", "5-6 days a week", "Daily"))) %>% 
  mutate(annual_income = factor(income_recode, 
                         levels = 1:4,
                         labels = c("<$50k", "$50K to <$100K", 
                                    "$100K to <$150K", ">150K "))) %>%
  # Collapse into binary variables  
  mutate(cannabis_use_binary = case_when(
    use_freq_rec %in% 1:3 ~ "Infrequent Usage", 
    use_freq_rec %in% 4:7 ~ "Frequent Usage",    
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  )) %>%

  mutate(income_binary = case_when(
    income_recode %in% 1:2 ~ "Lower Income", 
    income_recode %in% 3:4 ~ "Higher Income",  
    TRUE ~ NA_character_
  ))

states_map <- map_data("state")
states_map <- states_map %>%
  # Rename the spatial data column 
  mutate(State = state.abb[match(str_to_title(region), state.name)]) %>% 
  # Perform join based on shared column "State"
  left_join(pfas_births, by = "State") 
```

## *Color and Visual Elements*

When choosing colors for your visualizations these are some things to keep in mind:

-   Choose colors that are colorblind-friendly (avoid relying solely on red-green distinctions)
-   Use a consistent color palette throughout your analysis
-   Limit your color palette to 3-5 colors maximum to avoid visual clutter
-   Use color strategically to highlight key findings, not just for decoration
-   Ensure sufficient contrast between elements for readability

Let's look at an example for how to use color. First, let's explore all the color options available in R:

```{webr}
# These are the names of all the colours available in R.
colours()
```

Consider this map:

```{webr}
# PFAS Exposure US states map
ggplot(states_map, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, fill = mean_ppt)) + 
  geom_polygon(color = "black") + 
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "lightblue", high = "royalblue4", name = "Average PFAS Exposure by State (ppt)") +
  theme_map()
```

Compare the above version of the map with this one:

```{webr}
ggplot(states_map, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, fill = mean_ppt)) + 
  geom_polygon(color = "white") + 
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "lightgreen", high = "magenta", name = "Average PFAS Exposure by State (ppt)") +
  theme_map()
```

Referring to the beginning of this section, which map displays the data more meaningfully and is more visually appealing? Why?

## *Labeling Your Data and Text*

Having clear, concise labeling for your data components is crucial in making your visualization accessible.

1.  *Legends*

-   Place legends where they don't obstruct data points (usually top-right or bottom-right)
-   Use clear, descriptive legend titles and labels
-   Remove legends when they're redundant (it. only one data series)
-   Make legend text large enough to read easily

2.  *Axes and Scales*

-   Start y-axes at zero for bar charts to avoid misleading comparisons
-   Use appropriate scale breaks and intervals that make sense for your data
-   Label axes clearly with units of measurement included
-   Rotate x-axis labels if they're too long or crowded
-   Consider log scales for data spanning several orders of magnitude

3.  *Titles and Fonts*

-   Use readable font sizes
-   Keep titles concise but informative
-   Use consistent font throughout your visualization

Overall, and building on everything previously discussed, we want our data visualizations to convey meaningful information with as much organization and as little clutter as possible. Some final pointers:

-   Maintain adequate white space around your plot
-   Order categorical data logically (alphabetically, by frequency, or by meaningful hierarchy)
-   Remove unnecessary elements that don't add to understanding

## *Example 1*

If you saw this visualization given no context, you would not be able to make any sense of it for a variety of reasons.

Let's transform it step by step:

```{webr}
#| warning: false
total_pfas_data %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Value..PPT.)) + 
  
  # binwidth` or `bins`: size of the intervals the values fall under
  # fill: bar filled color
  # color: bar border color
  # alpha: color transparency
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "steelblue", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) + 
  
  # Creating appropriate axis units
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 100),
    breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 10), 
    labels = seq(0, 100, by = 10)
  ) +
  
  # Include informative and desrciptive titles and axes labels
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Total PFAS Measurements in Water Samples", #clear and descriptive title
    x = "Total PFAS Concentration (ppt)", 
    y = "Number of Samples" # Change 'count' to a more meaningful variable name 
  ) +
  
  # Minimalistic theme with no background annotations
  theme_minimal() +
  
  # Resize the text fonts
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    
    # Remove unnecessary clutter and minimize gridlines 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) 
  
```

## *Example 2*

Similarly, this visualization given no context would be hard to interpretate.

```{webr}
barplot(prop.table(table(cannabis_data$annual_income,
                         cannabis_data$recreational_use_frequency),
                   margin=2),
        legend.text=TRUE)
```

Let's transform it step by step:

```{webr}
# Clean the data
cannabis_plot_data <- cannabis_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(annual_income) & !is.na(recreational_use_frequency)) %>%
  count(annual_income, recreational_use_frequency) %>%
  group_by(recreational_use_frequency) %>%
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(cannabis_plot_data, aes(x = recreational_use_frequency, y = proportion, fill = annual_income)) +
  
  # Bars with heights according to y, white borders, and 0.8 width
  geom_col(color = "white", width = 0.8) +
  
  # Choose a good colour palette
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("#2c7bb6", "#abd9e9", "#fdae61", "#d7191c"),
    name = "Annual Income"
  ) +
  
  # Display proportion (y-axis) in % form
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.2),
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  
  # Include informative and desrciptive titles, axes labels, and caption
  labs(
    title = "Income Distribution by Cannabis Use Frequency",
    subtitle = "Stacked proportions showing income composition within each usage category",
    x = "Cannabis Use Frequency",
    y = "Proportion of Users (%)",
    caption = "Each bar represents 100% of users in that frequency category"
  ) +
  
  # Minimal theme feature
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    
    # Resize text font
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", margin = margin(b = 5)),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray60", margin = margin(b = 15)),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    
    # Adjust legend position, text font
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10),
    
    # Remove unnecessary clutter and minimize gridlines 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)
  )
```

# Conclusions

Congratulations! Through completion of learnR module 2, you have familiarized yourself with some R code that will be useful for lab assignments, and modified and ran R code to wrangle and summarize data.
